What I want to call your attention to here. Oh, private client group, they get stuff every month. And it's a mixed bag of stuff, sometimes more, sometimes less. There's some stuff everybody gets the same. There's some stuff each person gets just for them. And that's something you want to think about. It's sort of a logistical nightmare, but it's not awful. Especially if you can keep 15 or 20 or 34 people kind of over in a corner of your subconscious at all times. And if you're reading a magazine or you're reading a Wall Street Journal or you're going through your junk mail, your brain makes the connection. Oh, crap. Charlie should really see this. Because this is about a spa in Tupelo. He'll be really interested in this. Oh, Billy Bob should really see this. And then you need some kind of catcher system to get all that stuff organized. Mine's pretty simple, by the way. Everybody's got a drawer. And so that stuff accumulates in their drawer as the month goes by. And then we stick it all in a big envelope and we send it to them. And they get a big bag of stuff. What else was in here I want to show you. Here the next one, though, has some stuff. On 160, here's a kind of a typical memo to platinum. This is from 06. And this happens to be after there was a little kind of bad behavior at a meeting. So the last shall be first paragraph is corrective remedy. It's the three who took turns last at the last meeting because people left on them and bailed out on them. So they get to go first. And then we had a bunch of people who wouldn't take a turn when I wanted them to take a turn. So the new rule was if you pass when you're called on for a turn, you move all the way to last. So that's like a little corrective discipline. Now, keep in mind, these are 06's group. These are people paying $20,000 a year to be in the group. But it doesn't make any difference whether they paid you $20,000 or $2,000 or $200,000. Bad behaviors, bad behavior. And they've got to have their cookies and they're blanky taken away from them or something so that they behave better tomorrow. Up at the top, the top paragraph is dealing with next year's formation. And I would point out to you that this memo has a June 26 date on it starting to deal with 07 groups. A lot of people wait way too long if they have any kind of a renewal situation to deal with. They wait way too long to deal with the renewals. They're waiting until the last meeting. Well, you've got no second whack at them. You've got no peer pressure along the way. The last meeting's too late. You got to start to process a lot earlier than that. Page 162, interesting for one particular reason. This one, I don't know when it went out and I don't know what it's part of them. I don't know what the front part of it was. But this is earlier this year than in any prior year. I'm going to be sending out detailed information about my plans for 07 groups. And then the next paragraph is the unassigned time is fast disappearing, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. Now that whole paragraph, what that actually provokes is a immediate little rush of additional bookings for consulting and copywriting. I don't remember why I actually did it at the moment. I think I just drawn and bought a $75,000 horse if I'm not mistaken. But that whole paragraph of there's no time left and I really can't work anymore, et cetera. So what that really does is provoke a bunch of people now who hurry up and want you to do stuff. It's sort of primitive but effective reverse psychology. This is a little control memo and it's interesting literally only because of its first line. This is called negative optioning. It just makes your life easier. This is unless any of you has to be left out, I do that a lot where here's what I'm going to do. If you don't want me to do it or you don't want to be a part of it, you've got to now come back and tell me, otherwise it's happening. Then I don't have to wait for their responses. I don't have to worry about who hasn't responded. I don't have to chase answers. And I do it with a lot of stuff. I do it like if somebody sent in a testimonial and I'm going to use it in the next issue of the newsletter, I don't fax them and ask them to fax be back and tell me it was OK. I fax them and say, unless I hear you screaming sometime in the next 10 days, I'm using it.
